coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of 1,8-naphthalimide modified cellulose derivative composite fluorescent hydrogel probes and their application in the detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Zuo, Jingjing
Lv, Shenghua
Liang, Shan
Zhang, Shanshan
Wang, Jialin
Wei, Dequan
Liu, Leipeng
coredata.dc:description: The design and development of a rapid and quantitative method for the detection of heavy metal ions is of great importance for environmental protection. We have prepared a 1,8-Naphthalimide modified cellulose composite fluorescent hydrogel (CENAEA/PAA) with a stereo double network structure. Characterized by excellent hydrogel functional structure and fluorescence detection performance, it can efficiently and selectively identify and detect Cr(VI) with linear quenching in the range of 0–400 μmol/L and detection limit of 0.58 μmol/L for Cr(VI). The results show that the CENAEA/PAA can effectively adsorb Cr(VI) with a maximum adsorption capacity of 189.04 mg/g. Finally, the morphological characteristics, chemical structure, fluorescence properties and adsorption behavior of CENAEA/PAA were analyzed and fitted well with the pseudo-second-order model and Freundlich model. Thus, the present work provides a green and sustainable approach for the synthesis of a functional material that can be used for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 1,8-Naphthalimide
Modified cellulose
Composite hydrogel
Detection of Cr(VI)
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of NAEA and CENAEA
Selectivity of CENAEA/PAA for Cr(VI) detection
Fluorescence response of Cr(VI) to CENAEA/PAA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capability Guidance Program (No. 2021QFY04-04 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21276152 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of NAEA, CENAEA; (b) XRD images of CENAEA; (c, d) 1H, 13C NMR images of NAEA; (e) 1H NMR images of CENAEA.
(a, b) SEM images of CENAEA0/PAA; (c, d) SEM images of CENAEA0.05/ PAA.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CENAEA/PAA at different ratios; (b) UV spectra; (c-f) XPS spectra of CENAEA/PAA.
(a) Swelling ratio of CENAEA/PAA of different proportions; (b) Stress-strain (Insert: hydrogel stretching physical picture).
(a) Laser confocal image of CENAEA/PAA; (b,d) 3D fluorescence; (c) Excitation and emission, UV–vis absorption spectra; (e) Fluorescence intensity of CENAEA/PAA at different scales.
(a) Fluorescence responses of CENAEA/PAA to different metals; (b) Bar chart; (c) Physical drawings.
(a) Fluorescence response of CENAEA/PAA to different concentrations of Cr(VI); (b) Linear fit; (c) Time stability; (d) pH stability.
Cr(VI) adsorption and detection mechanism of CENAEA/PAA.
Fluorescence response of CENAEA/PAA to (a) mixed single metals; (b) mixed metals; (c) mixed organic solvents.

(a) Adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) at different times (Insert: fitting of pseudo-second- order model); (b) Adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) at different initial concentrations (Insert: Fitting of Langmuir model).

Maximum adsorption of Cr(VI) by hydrogels with different conditions. (a) CENAEA contents; (b) AA content; (c) Maximum adsorption of Cr(VI) by CENAEA/PAA under conditions of metal competition; (d) The reusability of CENAEA/PAA.
Synthesis of DAC, CENAEA and CENAEA/PAA.
Reagent dosage for different ratios of hydrogels.
Kinetic parameters of pseudo-first and pseudo-second order equations for Cr(VI) adsorption.
Langmuir and Freundlich model parameters for Cr(VI) adsorption.
Maximum adsorption capacity and detection limit compared to previous work.

row.entry: Fe3O4 NPs/ CS/ glyoxal/ PVA hydrogel

head.ce:title: Fabrication of 1,8-naphthalimide modified cellulose derivative composite fluorescent hydrogel probes and their application in the detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'’an 710021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design and development of a rapid and quantitative method for the detection of heavy metal ions is of great importance for environmental protection. We have prepared a 1,8-Naphthalimide modified cellulose composite fluorescent hydrogel (CENAEA/PAA) with a stereo double network structure. Characterized by excellent hydrogel functional structure and fluorescence detection performance, it can efficiently and selectively identify and detect Cr(VI) with linear quenching in the range of 0–400 μmol/L and detection limit of 0.58 μmol/L for Cr(VI). The results show that the CENAEA/PAA can effectively adsorb Cr(VI) with a maximum adsorption capacity of 189.04 mg/g. Finally, the morphological characteristics, chemical structure, fluorescence properties and adsorption behavior of CENAEA/PAA were analyzed and fitted well with the pseudo-second-order model and Freundlich model. Thus, the present work provides a green and sustainable approach for the synthesis of a functional material that can be used for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals can enter the human body through dermal contact, food consumption and inhalation and threaten the health of humans and animals. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is highly toxic and non-biodegradable, accumulating excessively in the body through bioaccumulation and causing acute damage [1–4]. Cr(VI) can interact with proteins and enzymes in the body, rendering them inactive and even harmful. The World Health Organization has proposed an interim guideline value for total chromium intake in humans of 0.05 mg/L. Technical methods historically used to detect hexavalent chromium include electrochemical techniques [5–8] and atomic absorption spectrometry [9,10]. However, these methods suffer from the disadvantages of expensive detection instruments, high price, and cumbersome detection, which greatly limit the scope of application. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop more rapid and quantitative methods for the determination of Cr(VI).

Among various detection methods, fluorescent sensors have been widely investigated for their high sensitivity and simplicity of equipment. Hydrogels have a three-dimensional network structure and are widely used in the field of fluorescent probes due to their high mechanical properties and abundant contact sites. Fluorescent hydrogels are new polymer composites with high recognition and detection capabilities, porous structures, rich chemical groups and fluorescence emission properties, which are of special interest in the recognition and detection of purification. A new polymer composite with fluorescence emission properties is capable of changing the fluorescence intensity or fluorescence emission color in response to the stimulation of an external target analyte. In addition, the presence of hydrophilic activation groups on the surface of the hydrogel matrix enables hydrogen bonding, adsorption or electrostatic chelation with Cr(VI) in wastewater, thus achieving the capture of heavy metal ions in water. Moreover, because hydrogels are water-insoluble and stable, they can be easily separated from wastewater, preventing recontamination. Because of these properties, hydrogels are attracting a lot of attention in the green field as a new type of heavy metal ion adsorbent. Zhang et al. [11] prepared a new PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel by copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomer for the detection of Fe3+. Yang et al. [12] synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon dot (NCD) hydrogel by low-temperature sintering to achieve sensitive detection of Pb2+ in water. Suvina et al. [13] investigated the formation of a novel hydrogel of the conjugated polymer polypyrrole (PPy) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) for the simultaneous detection of four different metal ions, such as Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+.

However, these hydrogels also have some disadvantages; artificial polymer hydrogels are usually non-biodegradable and biotoxic, and expensive nanomaterials are usually introduced to increase the adsorption capacity of heavy metal ions, thus limiting their use. On this basis, natural biomass-based materials, which are widely available, abundant, sustainably produced and environmentally friendly, are excellent raw materials for the preparation of heavy metal ion adsorption hydrogels. For example, cellulose-based hydrogels have been rapidly developed in recent years due to their hydrophilicity, reusability and biocompatibility, and have shown great potential in the field of heavy metal ion detection and adsorption. Li et al. [14] fabricated a new cellulose-based fluorescent hydrogel using a proportional probe with carbon dots as energy donors and rhodamine fraction as energy acceptors (CDs-Rho). The probe showed a sensitive linear response to Hg2+ in the range of 0–100 μmol/ L with a detection limit of 0.00219 μmol/L. Huang et al. [15] prepared a series of carbon-dotted (CD) cellulose hydrogels with different particle sizes and doped with various elements (O, N, S). Their detection limit for Hg2+ was 0.2 μmol/L in the range of 10–100 μmol/L. Peng et al. [16] prepared a fluorescent nanocellulose based hydrogel (FNH) incorporating titanate nanofibres (TNs) for the adsorption and detection of Cr(VI) ions. The maximum adsorption capacity and the minimum detection limit were 648.4 mg/g and 0.039 μg/L, respectively. In addition, the cellulose in cellulose-based hydrogels provides the “backbone” of the hydrogel to build a three-dimensional porous structure and provides many functional groups that act as chelating sites for heavy metal ions, while making the hydrogel material biocompatible. Therefore, cellulose is an ideal material for developing a novel hydrogel with the ability to detect and adsorb heavy metal ions.

Herein, a new type of 1,8-Naphthalimide fluorescent functional group was prepared, and 1,8-Naphthalimide modified cellulose was used as raw material to polymerize with acrylic acid under the action of initiator, and then a cross-linking agent was added to cross-link the chains to form 1,8-Naphthalimide modified cellulose composite fluorescent hydrogel CENAEA/PAA with a three-dimensional double network structure. The defects of the existing cellulose based hydrogels such as poor mechanical strength can be effectively improved. In addition, the hydrogel has a rich chemical group, three-dimensional porous structure, high recognition detection ability and adsorption effect for Cr(VI), combined with the advantages of degradability and good biocompatibility of cellulose, so the hydrogel has high practical application in the detection and adsorption of Cr(VI) removal.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), 1, 8-naphthalene anhydride (1,8-NA), Sodium Periodate were provided by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. anhydrous ethanol, ethylenediamine, sodium iodate, ethylene glycol, trichloromethane were provided by Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. N, N methylene diacrylamide (MBA), ammonium persulfate (APS), acrylic acid (AA), Ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), aluminium chloride (AlCl3), mercury sulphate (HgSO4), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), chromium trichloride (CrCl3), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were provided by Tianjin Comayo Chemical Reagent Co. Deionised water was used for all experiments.

S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM)(Hitachi, Japan), Edinburgh FS5 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK), Vertex 70 infrared spectrometer (Brueck, Germany), Lambda 25 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Perkinelmer), X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Kalpha), X-ray diffractometer (XRD)(Brueck, Germany), LSM800 laser confocal fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany), Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS) / High Performance Liquid Chromatography-Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (HPLC-ICP-MS) (Agilent, USA), AdvancedIII 600 MHz Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Hydrogen Spectrometer(Brueck, Germany), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller measurements (MicroActive for ASAP 2460 Version 2.01).

4 g of NaIO4 was dissolved in 80 mL of water and stirred to dissolve. Then 3 g of MCC was added under stirring, pH 3–4 was adjusted, and stirring was continued for 5 h under dark reaction conditions. Then 4 mL of ethylene glycol was added and stirring was continued for 1 h. The cellulose was filtered with water and ethanol to obtain diacetal cellulose (DAC).

1 g of 1,8-naphthalic anhydride was added to 60 mL of anhydrous ethanol and heated to 50 °C to dissolve, then 0.36 g of ethylenediamine solution was slowly added, the temperature was raised to 80 °C, stirred for 4 h, and cooled to 25 °C. After vacuum filtration, 1,8-Naphthalimide (NAEA) was prepared. NAEA: 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3) 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3) δ 8.68–8.42 (m, 1H), 8.34–7.99 (m, 1H), 7.75 (dd, J = 9.2, 5.5 Hz, 1H), 4.45–3.98 (m, 1H), 3.08 (td, J = 6.6, 2.7 Hz, 1H), 1.45 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (151 MHz, CDCl3) δ 164.48, 134.00, 133.99, 131.32, 131.30, 126.93, 122.56, 43.15, 40.53.

Disperse 0.5 g of DAC in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol, add 0.1 g of 1,8-Naphthalimide, and stir at 80 °C for 6 h. After cooling to 25 °C, the cellulose was extracted and filtered, and washed with anhydrous ethanol, trichloromethane to obtain 1,8-Naphthalimide modified cellulose (CENAEA).

S-4800 Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan) was used to examine the morphological characteristics of the samples at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Vertex 70 Infrared Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to analyse the chemical composition of the hydrogels in the region of 400–4000 cm−1. The Lambda 25 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Perkinelmer) was used to observe the introduction of fluorescent groups in the hydrogels. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Kalpha) was used to analyse the elemental composition of the hydrogels. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, Germany) was used to observe the crystal structure of the hydrogels. 1H NMR spectra and 13C NMR were recorded on the AdvancedIII 600 MHz Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Hydrogen Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with chemical shifts reported in ppm (in DMSO‑d 6 and CDCl3, TMS as internal standard) at room temperature. Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) and LSM800 laser confocal fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) were used to analyse the fluorescence properties of the hydrogels. Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS) / High Performance Liquid Chromatography-Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (HPLC-ICP-MS) (Agilent, USA) was used to test the adsorption properties of hydrogels on metal ions. The specific surface areas and pore diameter distributions of the prepared hydrogels were analyzed by using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller measurements (MicroActive for ASAP 2460 Version 2.01).

Fluorescence experiments were used to measure and calculate the fluorescence effects and detection limits of the hydrogel. In order to study the fluorescence selectivity of CENAEA/PAA hydrogel for Cr(VI), a solution of metal ions (Ni2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cr(VI)) at 0.01 mol/L concentration was prepared, and the hydrogel was cut into square chunks. The hydrogel was placed in several test tubes, 3.0 mL H2O was added, and then 100 μL of different metal ion solutions were added to each of them. The fluorescence emission spectra of different mixed systems at excitation wavelength of 348 nm were measured by fluorescence spectrometer, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity was recorded. To test the concentration effect of CENAEA/PAA, the hydrogels were immersed in different concentrations of Cr(VI) solution (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400 μmol/L), examined for changes in fluorescence intensity and linearly fitted. The limit of detection (LOD) for hydrogel was calculated as follows:
LOD = 3 δ / K
where δ is the standard deviation of the blank standard solution and K is the slope of the calibration curve.

Determination of Cr(VI) adsorption isotherms: the same volumes of Cr(VI) solutions with 100 mL different initial concentrations (50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 mg/L) were placed in conical flasks. CENAEA/PAA was added for the adsorption experiments. After 10 h of adsorption, the remaining solution was filtered using a filter membrane and measured using ICP-OES.

Determination of Cr(VI) adsorption kinetics: 100 mL of Cr(VI) solution (500 mg/L) was placed in a conical flask. CENAEA/PAA were added for adsorption experiments. Subsequently, a series of equilibration times (10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 300, 400 min) were performed for the adsorption kinetics. Finally, the remaining solution was filtered using a filter membrane and measured using ICP-OES.
Adsorption kinetics:

Adsorption efficiencies were evaluated separately from the proposed first and proposed second order models to understand the mechanism of their adsorption to hydrogels. The equations of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model were formulated as follows:
ln
Q e
−
Q t
= ln
Q e
−
k 1
t .

Pseudo-second-order kinetic model equation:
t
Q t
=
1
k 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
Where Q
e and Q
t are the adsorption capacity of fluorescent hydrogel at equilibrium time t and time t (mg/g), respectively. k
1 and k
2 are the rate constants of pseudo-first-order and pseudo- second-order dynamics.
Adsorption isotherm:

Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were used to describe the equilibrium data. The Langmuir isotherm for a saturated monolayer can be expressed as follows:
Q e
=
Q m
K L
C e
1 +
K L
C e
Where Q
m (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent surface, and K
L is the Langmuir constant. C
e and C
0 (mg/L) are the concentrations of Cr(VI) in equilibrium and initial solutions, respectively.

The Freundlich model is an empirical equation that assumes the presence of non-uniform adsorption energy on the adsorbent surface:
Q e
=
K F
C e
1 n
where K
F is Freundlich constant, representing the amount of adsorption on the adsorbent, and n is the dimensionless index of the Freundlich equation, indicating that the adsorption process is favorable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of NAEA and CENAEA
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR was used to determine the structures of MCC, DAC, NAEA and CENAEA, as shown in Fig. 1(a). It can be observed that compared with MCC, DAC has a stretching vibration peak of CO bond at 1724 cm−1 [17], which corresponds to the carbonyl group in DAC after the oxidation reaction of sodium periodate. As NAEA and CENAEA were modified by ethylenediamine, CN bond vibration peaks appeared at 1587 cm−1 [18]. At the same time, after the synthesis of CENAEA by NAEA grafting on DAC, the vibration peaks of OH and CN appeared at 3463 cm−1 and 2910 cm−1, respectively, indicating the successful synthesis of CENAEA [19].

XRD were carried out on MCC, DAC and CENAEA respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(b). It can be seen that the MCC shows three typical characteristic peaks at 2θ = 15.1°, 22.2° and 34.5°, corresponding to the crystal structures of (110), (200) and (004), respectively, representing the structural features of cellulose type I [20,21]. After successive oxidation by sodium periodate and modification by ethylenediamine, both DAC and CENAEA showed typical characteristic peaks at 15.1°, 22.2° and 34.5° with no significant change in orientation and shape, but the intensity of the peaks weakened slightly, while the peak at 22.2° became weaker, probably due to the destruction of the crystal structure caused by the oxidation of sodium periodate. In addition, Fig. 1 (c, d, e) shows the NMR results of NAEA and CENAEA. In the 1H NMR spectra of NAEA, the region between 1.0 and 5.0 ppm corresponds to the alkyl hydrogen proton peaks in the structure of NAEA formed by the reaction of 1,8-naphthalic anhydride with ethylenediamine, and the region between 7.0 and 9.0 ppm corresponds to the aromatic hydrogen proton peaks. Similarly, in the 1H NMR spectra of CENAEA obtained by modifying cellulose with NAEA, the regions between 1.0 and 5.0 ppm corresponded to the alkyl hydrogen proton peaks and the hydrogen proton peaks of cellulose glucose units, and the aromatic proton peaks of naphthalene rings appeared in the range of 7.5 to 9.0 ppm, which indicated that the NAEA grafted cellulose material had been successfully prepared.

Polyacrylic acid (PAA) chains are often used as a good matrix for hydrogel networks because they are rich in carboxyl groups. Therefore, in the preparation of CENAEA/PAA hydrogels, the hydrogen bonding interactions between CENAEA and PAA chains form a cross-linked network. At the same time, due to the preparation process, acrylic acid is mixed with modified cellulose CENAEA for polymerization and cross-linking, so some of the PAA formation process will be covalent cross-linking with cellulose [22]. Therefore, there are hydrogen bonding interactions and covalent cross-linking in the CENAEA-PAA crosslinked network, which provides an excellent three-dimensional gel structure for the synthesis of cellulose hydrogels with good sensing and adsorption properties. The surface morphologies of CENAEA0/PAA and CENAEA0.05/PAA were characterized by SEM. It can be seen from Fig. 2 that the synthesized hydrogels all have three-dimensional network porous structures. Compared with CENAEA0/PAA, the pore structures of different sizes in CENAEA0.05/PAA are interconnected and evenly distributed, and have a denser network structure, which is due to the effective cross-linking of MCC and CENAEA fluorophore. It can lay a foundation for the subsequent efficient adsorption and fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) in water. Furthermore, the specific surface area and pore size of CENAEA/PAA was determined using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller manner. The specific surface area of the CENAEA/PAA was about 2.368 m2/g, the pore size of CENAEA/PAA is mainly distributed around 2.002 nm. At this pore size and specific surface area, the metal ions enter the interior of the hydrogel through the pores and adsorb the metal ions, achieving electrostatic adsorption or chelation between the hydrogel and the metal ions, and ultimately producing an adsorption effect. Therefore, the prepared CENAEA0.05/PAA can provide many effective Cr(VI) adsorption sites and ion mass transfer channels, thus improving the adsorption, detection and adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) in water.

The hydrogels at different ratios were analyzed using FTIR and it can be seen from Fig. 3(a) that the hydrogel systems show similar trends and stretching vibrations of the OH and CO functional groups at 3482 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 respectively. And the characteristic peaks of CN and CN bonds appeared at 2852 cm−1 and 1314 cm−1. When the amount of CENAEA added was 0, almost no characteristic peaks appeared, while the hydrogels with CENAEA added all showed small characteristic peaks, and the characteristic peaks gradually increased with the increase of CENAEA content. This proves the successful introduction of CENAEA in hydrogel systems. The UV/Vis spectra of the 1,8-naphthalenediyldiamine modified cellulose fluorescent hydrogels are shown in Fig. 3(b), from which it can be seen that the hydrogel without CENAEA has only one characteristic absorption peak at 289 nm. This is presumed to be a π → π* leap in the aromatic sp2 orbital, whereas the hydrogel with CENAEA added shows a new absorption peak at 346 nm, presumably an n → π* leap in the CENAEA fluorescent group, thus indicating the successful introduction of the fluorescent group.

In order to determine the chemical composition of 1, 8-naphthalenediamine modified cellulose fluorescent hydrogels, CENAEA/PAA were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3(c), the image has distinct peaks at 283, 399, and 531 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The atomic content of the elements C, N and O is 64.54 % (C), 3.21 % (N) and 32.25 % (O). The spectra of C1s (Fig. 3d) show that at 284.76 eV, 286.57 eV, 287.95 eV and 288.92 eV correspond to the CNC, COC, CO and O=C-OH bonds of the hydrogel [23]. The presence of NH (400.02 eV), CN (401.61 eV), and (C)3 N (402.32 eV) were confirmed by the spectra of N1s (Fig. 3e) [24]. The O 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 3(f) can be divided into two peaks at 531.91 eV and 533.15 eV, indicating the presence of CO and CO [25]. Based on FTIR, UV–vis and XPS, the successful synthesis of 1, 8-naphthalenediamine modified cellulose fluorescent hydrogel was obtained.

The equilibrium swelling rate is one of the important parameters to evaluate the performance of gel materials. Fig. 4(a) shows the swelling performance of 1,8-naphthalenediyl ethylene diamine modified cellulose fluorescent hydrogels. The swelling rate of the gels with different CENAEA contents increased sharply from 0 to 20 h, then increased slowly and reached equilibrium after 24 h [26–28]. Compared to the hydrogel without the addition of CENAEA, the equilibrium swelling rate of the hydrogel with the addition of CENAEA was significantly reduced. This is mainly due to the fact that the added CENAEA becomes the cross-linking point in the hydrogel, resulting in an increase in the cross-link density of the hydrogel, which in turn leads to a decrease in the swelling rate and equilibrium swelling rate of the hydrogel. Among them, it can be seen that the CENAEA content of 0.05 g has better swelling properties and the spatial network structure has a high cross-link density, which results in better mechanical properties. The tensile stress-strain curve of the hydrogel is shown in Fig. 4(b). The tensile strength of the hydrogel without CENAEA reaches 0.32 MPa, while the tensile strength of the hydrogel with CENAEA can reach 0.42 MPa. This is because the cross-link density of the hydrogel increases with the addition of CENAEA, resulting in improved mechanical properties and therefore higher mechanical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CENAEA/PAA for Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a) and (b) show the fluorescence images of CENAEA/PAA under a laser confocal fluorescence microscope. It can be seen that CENAEA/PAA has an intense blue fluorescence with a uniform fluorescence distribution. In addition, the fluorescence behavior of CENAEA/PAA was investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the spectra of the CENAEA/PAA exhibited a UV visible absorption band at 346 nm and a fluorescence emission peak at 355 nm. Combined with Fig. 5c and Fig. S5, it can be seen that the absorption peak at 346 nm of the CENAEA/PAA hydrogel increases significantly with the addition of Cr(VI), which may be attributed to the enhancement of the UV absorption here due to the complexation of the functional groups in the hydrogel with Cr(VI). According to Fig. 5(d), it can be concluded that CENAEA/PAA has a stronger fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. And from Fig. 5(e), it can be seen that the hydrogel has stronger fluorescence at 0.05 g CENAEA content. Therefore, combining the swelling properties, tensile properties and fluorescence intensity of CENAEA/PAA, it was finally decided to use CENAEA0.05/PAA in the subsequent experiments. However, it can be seen that the emission peaks of CENAEA0.1/PAA are shifted. The fluorescence emission peak shift is due to different fluorescence phenomena in the monomeric and aggregated states. Fluorescent molecules have different degrees of blue shift in their fluorescence spectra in the monomeric and aggregated states. When the CENAEA content was 0.1 g, only CENAEA monomer existed in the system. And when the CENAEA content was 0.02, 0.05 and 0.08 g, both MCC and CENAEA existed in the system, and they existed in the aggregation state. And in the aggregated state, the wavelength blue shift in the aggregated state occurs due to the change in the distribution of charge density in the interacting molecules, which causes the fluorescence peaks to shift to shorter wavelengths. Additionally, in order to explore the quenching mechanism, the average fluorescence lifetime of the CENAEA/PAA without Cr(VI) and with Cr(VI) was also examined (Fig. S1), the results were 6.23 ns and 5.61 ns. Apparently, the addition of Cr(VI) did not significantly shorten the fluorescence lifetime of the CENAEA/PAA, which proved that the existence of the static quenching process [29,30].

To examine the fluorescence response of CENAEA/PAA to different metal ions, they were immersed in 300 μmol/L (100 μL of 0.01 mol/L different metal solutions in 3 mL H2O) of different metal ion solutions for a period of time, and the fluorescence intensity of CENAEA/PAA was measured after sufficient reaction [31]. Fig. 6(a) and (b) show that the effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence intensity of CENAEA/PAA was the most significant, with a significant weakening of the fluorescence intensity. In contrast, the quenching effect of other metal ions was close to that of the blank without the addition of metal ions, with a weaker quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Cr(VI) to CENAEA/PAA
ce:section.ce:para: After concluding that Cr(VI) had a good quenching effect on the CENAEA/PAA, Cr(VI) was added at different concentration gradients (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400 μmol/L) and analyzed again. From Fig. 7(a), it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels gradually decreased with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration. When the Cr(VI) concentration reached 400 μmol/L, the fluorescence of CENAEA/PAA was basically quenched. Meanwhile, based on the fluorescence intensity of CENAEA/PAA before and after the addition of Cr(VI), the fluorescence responsivity was calculated to be 0.91, which has a good fluorescence detection effect. Stern-Volmer equation was also used to analyse the change of fluorescence intensity of CENAEA/PAA, and the linear eq. F/F0 = 0.7543–0.0017C[Cr(VI)] (R2 = 0.9916) (The fluorescence intensity of hydrogel at 400 nm is F0, the fluorescence intensity of hydrogel Cr(VI) at 400 nm is F) was obtained. The fluorescence response and detection limit of Cr(VI) on hydrogels with different CENAEA contents were examined, and the results are shown in Table S1. With the increase of CENAEA content, the detection limit showed a trend of decreasing and then increasing, with the lowest detection limit at 0.05 CENAEA, the concentration of Cr(VI) is in the range of 0–400 μmol/L, and the detection limit is 0.58 μmol/L [32–34]. After adding Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity can be changed rapidly within 2 min to achieve the effect of fluorescence burst. The hydrogels with different AA contents were also tested for fluorescence performance, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. and Table S2., the hydrogels showed different adsorption properties and fluorescence detection properties at AA contents of 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 mL, respectively. Among them, when the AA content was 3 mL, the detection limit was lower. At the same time, the change of fluorescence intensity of CENAEA on Cr(VI) was analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation, and the linear eq. F/F0 = 0.6302–0.0016C[Cr(VI)] (R2 = 0.9128) was obtained, with the detection limit of 0.64 μmol/L. The fluorescence response of CENAEA to different metal ions can be analyzed in Fig. S3., which showed that the CENAEA/PAA hydrogel has better sensitivity and specific selectivity for Cr(VI). Therefore, the introduction of CENAEA fluorophore into the hydrogel, a flexible medium material, can lead to more excellent fluorescence performance.

To investigate the stability and fluorescence responsiveness of CENAEA/PAA, we performed fluorescence spectroscopy for a period of time after the addition of a quantitative Cr(VI) solution, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(c). The fluorescence intensity diminished immediately after the addition of Cr(VI) and then remained stable. The CENAEA/PAA remain stable in the range of 0–30 min and have a strong fluorescence stability. Therefore, CENAEA/PAA prepared in this experiment theoretically meet the requirements for rapid industrial detection. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of the CENAEA/PAA were investigated for Cr(VI) bursting in different pH systems [35]. The effect of pH on the PL properties of CENAEA/PAA hydrogels was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the CENAEA/PAA showed good stability in the pH range from 1 to 12. Apparently, the fluorescence intensity gradually grows with increasing pH from 1 to 6, reaching a maximum value at pH = 6, while the fluorescence intensity remains stable at pH 6–13. This pH-dependent PL behavior may be due to the dissociation of a large number of imine groups on the surface of the fluorophore substance. Acidic environments have a great influence on CENAEA/PAA hydrogels because imine bonds are easily broken in acidic environments, which leads to the unstable fluorescence properties of these hydrogels. The results show that CENAEA/PAA are stable over a wide pH range and can be better applied in practice.

In practical applications where Cr(VI) is detected, the environment may contain multiple metal ions or a variety of different solvent systems. In order to simulate and explore the effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence quenching of CENAEA/PAA in a real environment, experiments were carried out to investigate the effect of mixed metal ions. Different metal ions (Ni2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+) at a concentration of 0.01 mol/L was configured and the fluorescence intensity was detected by first adding 100 μL of the other metal ions except Cr(VI) to the hydrogel, and then adding 100 μL Cr(VI) solution to obtain a mixed metal solution with a Cr(VI) concentration of 300 μmol/L, and the fluorescence intensity was detected again. Experiments on the effects of different metal mixture solutions on the fluorescence performance of CENAEA/PAA were also carried out. The fluorescence detection results were analyzed and the results are shown in Fig. 9(a) and (b). Whether it is a single metal or multiple metals, CENAEA/PAA still selectively detects Cr(VI) with a high level of interference resistance under competing conditions with different metal ions. This demonstrates the specific selection of CENAEA/PAA for Cr(VI) under conditions of competition for different metal ions and its applicability in a practical detection environment. Meanwhile, the effect of Cr(VI) on CENAEA/PAA in different solvent systems (distilled water, tap water, EtOH, MeOH, DMF) was explored to simulate the actual environment and the effect of hydrogel fluorescence intensity was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 9(c). After the addition of Cr(VI), the solvent system shows a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity, so that CENAEA/PAA can still selectively detect Cr(VI) in different solvent systems. In summary, CENAEA/PAA is still effective for the detection of Cr(VI) in a practical environment.

Then the adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) was studied. Firstly, to investigate the effect of contact time on the adsorption capacity, pseudo-first and pseudo-second order equations were used, as shown in Fig. 10 (a) and Fig. S6. Within the first 20 min, the adsorption capacity of the CENAEA/PAA for Cr(VI) reached approximately 90 % of the equilibrium adsorption capacity. After that, until the adsorption equilibrium is reached, the increase of the adsorption capacity gradually slows down. The pseudo-second-order model can be used to describe the experimental data with a good correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9987 and the kinetic parameters for Cr(VI) are shown in Table 2. The applicability of the second order rate equation to the available data suggests that the adsorption process of Cr(VI) is mainly controlled by the chemisorption process. Fig. 10 (b) and Fig. S7. show the isotherms of Cr(VI) adsorption by CENAEA/ PAA. As the initial concentration of heavy metal ions increases, the adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) progressively increases and finally stabilises. The equilibrium data were fitted using the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm models and the relevant parameters are shown in Table 3. In addition, the maximum adsorption capacity of the CENAEA/PAA for Cr(VI) simulated by the Langmuir model was 189.04 mg/g [38,39]. Table 4 also summarizes the maximum adsorption capacities and detection limits for the other hydrogels. There are significant differences between these maximum adsorption capacities. The new fluorescent cellulose-based hydrogel proved to be a valuable potential adsorbent.

The maximum adsorption of Cr(VI) by hydrogels with different CENAEA contents was tested and the results are shown in Table S3. and Fig. 11(a). The hydrogels with different AA contents were also tested for adsorption, and the results were shown in Table S4 and Fig. 11(b), with higher adsorption when AA was 3 mL. In addition, the adsorption of Cr(VI) by CENAEA/PAA under the competition of metal ions were tested. Firstly, we tested the adsorption of CENAEA/PAA on individual metal ions such as Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and then we mixed Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Cr(VI) in the same solution. As shown in Fig. 11(c), the adsorption effect of CENAEA/PAA on other metal ions was poor, and the maximum adsorption amount of 185.58 mg/g in the solution of mixed metal ions was about the same as that under normal conditions, indicating that the hydrogel still had a better ability to resist interference when adsorbing Cr(VI). In order to fulfill the requirements for sustainable development, CENAEA/PAA hydrogels were tested for reproducibility. Desorption was achieved by immersing the adsorbed hydrogels in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) solution, followed by cyclic testing. The desorption-adsorption process in 500 mg/L Cr(VI) solution for different cycling times is shown in Fig. 11(d). After five cycles, the prepared hydrogel still has a high adsorption capacity, indicating that the hydrogel has good reusability. In addition, we also investigated the mechanical properties of hydrogels after reuse and the results are shown in Fig. S8. From the results, it can be seen that the hydrogel is still able to reach a tensile strength between 0.1 and 0.2 MPa and an elongation at break of 103 % after 5 times of reuse, which indicates that the mechanical properties of the hydrogel maintain good mechanical properties although there is a slight decrease in the process of reuse.

In this paper, a novel composite double network fluorescent hydrogel was prepared by grafting naphthalimide fluorophore (NAEA) onto modified cellulose and then cross-linking CENAEA with acrylic acid in situ. The hydrogel quenched Cr(VI) in the linear range from 0 to 400 μmol/L with a detection limit of 0.58 μmol/L. In addition, the adsorption equilibrium isotherms and kinetic results showed that the adsorption of the new cellulose-based fluorescent hydrogel followed the Freundlich and pseudo-second-order models with a maximum adsorption of 189.04 mg/g for Cr(VI). The morphology, chemical structure, synthesis mechanism and optical properties of CENAEA/PAA were investigated. Experimental results showed that the addition of CENAEA could form porous structures and mass transfer channels, providing more binding sites and fast optical response to Cr(VI), thus significantly improving the adsorption and detection of Cr(VI). Therefore, the new cellulose-based fluorescent hydrogels, with their high adsorption capacity and ability to detect Cr(VI), have excellent potential for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.
Jingjing Zuo: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Software, Writing-original draft, Review, Editing.
Shenghua Lv: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition, Review, Project administration.
Shan Liang: Investigation, Data curation.
Shanshan Zhang: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation.
Jialin Wang: Investigation.
Dequan Wei: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-review & editing, Review.
Leipeng Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis.

Guo, Fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating Amino-Functionalized Fe3O4 and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr (VI), Colloid Surface A 658 (2023) 130673. doi:10.1016/ j.colsurfa.2022.130673.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr(VI)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.Y. Luo, X.H. Huang, Y. Luo, H.M. Yuan, T.T. Ren, X.J. Li, D. Xu, X. Guo, Y.Q. Wu, Fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr(VI), Chem. Eng. J. 407 (2021) 127050, doi:10.1016/j.cej.2020.127050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots crosslinked cellulose nanofibril/chitosan interpenetrating hydrogel system for sensitive detection and efficient adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Q. Chen, Z.H. Song, B.N. Yuan, X.J. Li, S.P. Li, T.T. Nguyen, M.H. Guo, Z.H. Guo, Fluorescent carbon dots crosslinked cellulose Nanofibril/Chitosan interpenetrating hydrogel system for sensitive detection and efficient adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI), Chem. Eng. J. 430 (4) (2022) 133154, doi:10.1016/j.cej.2021.133154.
